item.title: Politics
Trump’s Tariff War Has Failed on Every Front
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s tariffs and the uncertainty surrounding them have sent shockwaves through the global economy but have failed to achieve any of their stated objectives. Restoring an open, multilateral trading system will not be easy, but eliminating his arbitrary measures will be a necessary first step.

item.title: Libya, Iran, and the Limits of Airpower
item.description: What a previous intervention reveals about the hard choices that U.S. President Donald Trump faces.

item.title: How Much AI-Driven Productivity Growth Do We Want?
item.description: As AI advances, societies must consider how to strike a balance between the disruption caused by rapid productivity growth and its many benefits, including rising incomes and living standards. Fortunately, most advanced economies are well equipped to absorb the shocks associated with breakthrough technologies.

item.description: Its lead over the West is only set to widen

item.title: What’s Next for Cuba?
item.description: From Venezuela to Iran, US President Donald Trump’s administration has apparently embraced a simple mantra: attack now, plan later. In Cuba’s case, the attack has so far taken the form not of airstrikes, but of blockade on fuel imports, which has produced a severe economic and humanitarian crisis. But while Trump speaks glibly of the Cuban regime’s “imminent demise” and a “friendly takeover” of the country, he has offered no vision for the island’s future, though he may have created a blueprint for China to apply in Taiwan.

item.title: The Big Picture
Where Have All the Allies Gone?
item.description: The multilateral system that US President Donald Trump has dismantled was not, as he believes, a trap laid by weaker states to fleece a credulous superpower. It was infrastructure that took decades to build – and whose wreckage lies in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Iran’s Civilizational Rhetoric Is Hollow
item.description: The Islamic Republic claims to be thinking in terms of centuries, but its actions suggest otherwise.

item.title: The Responsibility to Protect and the War Against Iran
item.description: The US-Israeli war on Iran has fueled allegations that the Responsibility to Protect doctrine created a permissive environment for military intervention. But R2P was designed to address only mass-atrocity crimes – and to ensure restraint, legitimacy, and accountability when the international community confronts their perpetrators.

item.title: New research uncovers more of the story of man’s best friend
item.description: The evolutionary tale of Europe’s dogs is becoming clearer

item.description: Such a view both overstates and understates the scope of Beijing’s ambitions.

item.title: The Battle to End Palestinian Self-Determination
item.description: While the world focuses on the Iran war, Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank are acutely vulnerable.

item.title: As Cybercrime Soars in Central Asia, Digital Literacy Lags Behind
item.description: There’s no easy solution, but the most obvious first step is to empower individuals to think critically about what they read online.

item.title: The Wrong Choice in Cuba
item.description: US President Donald Trump, like the 1960s-era CIA, is trying to bring down Cuba's communist government by decapitating it, albeit with less extravagant methods than poisoned cigars and exploding seashells. But such schemes did not work back then, and simply removing Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel will not work now.

item.title: Iran’s Peace Negotiator Is His Country’s Trump
item.description: Mohammad Ghalibaf’s long rise was fueled by populist imagery, real estate corruption, and ruthlessness.

item.title: Canada Faces Its Finland Moment
item.description: Like Helsinki during the Cold War, Ottawa today is threatened by a bellicose and powerful neighbor.

item.title: Denmark’s Political Tightrope
item.description: At a time when many European countries are struggling to cope with the challenge posed by political polarization and fragmentation, Denmark under Mette Frederiksen’s government appeared to be an exception. But the just-completed election suggests otherwise.

item.description: Genomics suggests the country has been making wine for over 2,500 years

item.description: Bangladesh won its maritime boundary a decade ago and has barely drilled a single well.

item.description: Both public sentiment and political trends in Japan mean that a firm approach toward China is likely to endure.

item.description: Faced with another energy crisis, the continent is reaching for a form it had largely abandoned.

item.title: Lee Jae-myung Stars in Seoul’s Mayoral Race
item.description: This year's local elections are becoming a contest for presidential favor. That's a symptom of South Korea’s longstanding personality-driven politics.

item.title: Donald Trump’s Suez Moment
item.description: In 1956, British and French leaders launched an operation to overthrow a hostile government in Egypt and restore their own countries' global preeminence, only to suffer a humiliating defeat that would leave them weakened and dependent on the United States. Is the US president dragging his country down a similar path in Iran?

item.title: Beyond the Strait: Iran’s Expanding Reach Into the Indo-Pacific
item.description: Indo-Pacific planners need to revisit their Iran assumptions.

item.title: The world’s most unaffordable housing is not where you think
item.description: Asia’s great cities will founder without more reasonably priced homes

item.title: US-Style Health Care Is Wrong for the UK
item.description: When it comes to health-care systems, America serves as a cautionary tale, not as an instruction manual. If the British government moves toward a US-style system by subsidizing private insurance, as Reform UK leader Nigel Farage has proposed, its health outcomes will suffer markedly.

item.description: The secretary of defense fails to understand that rules of engagement benefit the U.S. military.

item.description: Moscow’s missteps offer a warning—and an opening—for Washington.

item.description: A pliant autocracy in Iran won’t solve America’s problems in the region.

item.title: America’s War, America’s Recession
item.description: The precise magnitude of the shock the US economy will face as a result of its war of choice in Iran is difficult to predict, given the array of factors at play. But the US is poorly equipped to weather the likely effects on inflation and interest rates, and financial-sector fragility is a key reason why.

item.title: As an AI Scholar, I Am Now Putting a High Probability on an AI Doomsday
item.description: What happens when the house of cards collapses?

item.title: This Energy Shock Demands a Green Industrial Strategy
item.description: In an era of geopolitical tumult, economic resilience requires changing not only the kinds of energy we consume, but also how, where, and by whom things are produced. Fortunately, through mission-oriented green industrial strategies, governments can help secure living standards and build economic resilience simultaneously.

item.title: India, China, and How Not to Save the Brahmaputra
item.description: Instead of pursuing a diplomatic solution to manage shared waters, India is following a dam-for-dam policy.

item.description: The Pentagon’s new $200 billion private equity fund would harm the critical industries it aims to support.

item.description: Addressing the 15th SPA, Kim enshrined permanent enmity with Seoul and declared his regime “ready for any choice” from the United States.

item.title: US Institutional Decay Is Threatening Global Finance
item.description: Even through tumultuous political cycles, the Federal Reserve, the Securities and Exchange Commission, and the Federal Trade Commission have been able to signal that US markets operate under clear, impersonal, reliably enforced rules. But this is no longer true, and the implications for the rest of the world are dire.

item.description: The three horrific murders of Uzbek women in Turkiye shed harsh light on the risks female migrant workers face, usually at the hands of those closest to them.

item.title: A Golden Opportunity for a Beleaguered WTO
item.description: By adopting the Investment Facilitation for Development Agreement, the World Trade Organization would brighten developing economies’ future prospects at a time of rising economic uncertainty. This would also send a powerful signal that, despite the challenges it faces, the WTO can still deliver tangible results.

item.description: A strategic location coupled with a close U.S. partnership makes for an attractive military target.

item.title: The Far Right’s False Pacifism Is Endangering Europe
item.description: Europe's nationalist parties talk endlessly about defending European countries, but they are the least willing to confront hostile foreign governments. By taking a weak security posture, these leaders are practically inviting other countries to escalate their threats to Europe’s way of life.

item.description: Even once the war ends, it will take time to get back to normal energy flows.

item.description: The conservative and right side of Australian politics has fractured completely.

item.title: In Kashmir, Iran’s War Is Personal
item.description: When Kashmiri Shias donate heirlooms for war-hit Iran, they are living out 700 years of shared civilization – and creating a headache for New Delhi.

item.title: What Is America’s Goal in Cuba?
item.description: With the US fuel blockade creating a humanitarian crisis, Cuba’s current stalemate with the United States cannot last indefinitely. But it remains to be seen whether the Trump administration will focus solely on opening the market or insist that economic reforms are accompanied by political liberalization.

item.title: Is Honesty the Best Policy for the International Order?
item.description: Instead of ensuring greater consistency in the application of global rules or inspiring reforms to existing institutions, Western leaders’ newfound frankness about the international order seems to be advancing quite different ends. If this is where honesty leads, we may soon miss the "fictional" world we have lost.

item.title: What Trump Gets Wrong About the Cultural Logic Driving Iran
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s Iran strategy rests on the premise that economic and military force will eventually compel the regime to back down. But such approaches tend to backfire in societies where preservation of honor and reputation dictates defiance.

item.title: Solving the Tuberculosis Puzzle
item.description: A new class of rapid molecular tests for TB that can be deployed far closer to where people first seek care could significantly close the diagnostic gap and slow the spread of the world’s deadliest infectious disease. But harnessing the tests' transformative potential requires significant political will.

item.title: Temirov Live Director Makhabat Tazhibek kyzy Released Pending New Trial
item.description: Although the Kyrgyz Supreme Court had previously upheld the verdict against her, Tazhibek kyzy was granted a new trial and released on parole this week.

item.title: Trapped in the Gulf
item.description: Tens of thousands of seafarers wait with fear and boredom as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

item.title: BTS Returned to Gwanghwamun. Not Everyone in South Korea Was Celebrating.
item.description: The Korean government treated a BTS comeback concert as a national event. The response at home was more complicated than expected.

item.title: Russia Is the Biggest Winner of the Iran War
item.description: While the reasons US President Donald Trump has given for the war against the Islamic Republic do not hold up, it is already clear that Russian President Vladimir Putin stands to gain the most from it. With oil prices surging, Russia is poised to reap an enormous windfall that will help sustain its own war against Ukraine.

item.title: Iran Is Becoming America’s Ukraine
item.description: The shift to an attritional war focused on energy infrastructure risks becoming a quagmire.

item.title: Iran’s Biggest Wartime Advantage Is Geography
item.description: The shape of the war is being determined by the country’s rugged terrain and vast coastline.

item.title: A New Opening for WTO Reform
item.description: World Trade Organization members agree that structural reform is necessary, but they diverge on what that should look like. A good first step would be for the trade ministers attending the WTO’s 14th Ministerial Conference in Yaoundé, Cameroon, to recommit the trade system to an overarching focus on sustainable development.

item.title: What Honda’s EV Retreat Means for the Future of Clean Tech
item.description: Japan’s carmakers have treated battery-electric vehicles as niche, betting instead on hybrids, hydrogen and e-fuels. Now, China, with its EVs and batteries as core industries, is set to define the future of mobility.

item.title: The War on Oil: Iran Conflict and the Global Energy Crisis
item.description: Adi Imsirovic has 35 years of experience in global energy markets. He says the current disruption to the Strait of Hormuz represents the gravest energy shock of his career.

item.description: Beijing’s long-term ambitions are increasingly at odds with its short-term reluctance to assume responsibility.

item.description: The first session of the 15th Supreme People's Assembly served as a definitive declaration that nuclear weapons remain a permanent necessity for the survival of the Kim regime.

item.description: Six of the 11 nations of Southeast Asia are making a concerted effort to carve out a role for themselves in the semiconductor industry. Which ones are actually succeeding?

item.title: The Gulf Is Becoming Uninsurable
item.description: The Gulf states never anticipated Iran’s strategy of pressuring the US by targeting them, because they never anticipated US indifference to their security. Their reputation for safety and stability in tatters, they will pay a heavy price.

item.description: How long can a small, vulnerable country continue to balance between adversaries if the war widens further and the pressure to choose sides intensifies?

item.description: After a long delay, the upcoming polls will determine whether the institutions created by the peace agreement can sustain a durable transition to peace and democratic governance.

item.description: When voters sense the president is more focused abroad than on the economy, they punish him for it.

item.description: How Europe and the United States could end up in a healthier alliance.

item.description: In a world increasingly shaped by rivalries between major continental powers, new wars, and powerful proprietary technologies, scale matters more than ever. Yet the European economy remains too fragmented, structured as a collection of national markets rather than as one continental bloc.

item.title: Beijing’s Real Problem With Trump’s China Summit Delay
item.description: In the end, a postponement may suit Beijing’s interests more than Washington’s – as long as it doesn’t become linked with the Hormuz issue.

item.title: War in Iran Could Close Central Asia’s Gateway to the Sea
item.description: Over the past decade, analysts have increasingly viewed Iranian transit routes as Central Asia’s most promising path to the sea.

item.title: Why the Digital Euro Needs Worker Input
item.description: The proposed digital euro represents a critical opportunity for Europe to advance digital sovereignty and fair competition. But to gain the trust of European citizens and businesses, the European Central Bank and other stakeholders must consult with the workers who will be responsible for day-to-day implementation.

item.title: Trump Is Spending Tomorrow’s Security Today
item.description: Neither incompetence nor impulsiveness explains Donald Trump’s decision to start a war despite warnings that stocks of crucial weapons were badly depleted. Across his presidency, in domain after domain – fiscal policy, science funding, alliance management, and more – present consumption comes at the expense of future capacity.

item.title: The Indonesia-US Agreement: A ‘Reciprocal’ Trade Deal That Isn’t
item.description: In the 2026 Indonesia-U.S. Agreement on Reciprocal Trade, the phrase “Indonesia shall” appears more than 200 times. “United States shall” appears in just nine. The agreement may not hold up under international law.

item.title: Iranian Kurds Can Fight, but How Effectively?
item.description: Political concerns over Kurdish involvement overlook more pressing practical issues.

item.title: Trump’s Hormuz Warship Request Puts South Korea-US Alliance to the Test
item.description: President Lee Jae-myung faces a difficult dilemma as he seeks to uphold the South Korea-U.S. alliance while avoiding entanglement in a drawn-out war against Iran.

item.title: Ukraine and the EU Need a Fresh Start
item.description: “Membership lite” could clear the hurdles to Kyiv’s EU accession.

item.title: Taiwan’s First PRC-born Legislator Stokes Controversy
item.description: The status of Chinese spouses of Taiwanese has been increasingly a topic of debate in Taiwan.

item.title: China Funds 9 Border Posts for Tajikistan as Frontier Violence Escalates
item.description: The $61 million grant fits a decade-long pattern of Chinese security investment – now accelerating after attacks on Chinese nationals.

item.title: Who’s Whispering in Your Chatbot’s Ear?
item.description: Liberal democracies are setting the stage for techno-authoritarian drift by giving private companies centralized, unaccountable power over AI infrastructure. Given the far-reaching social and political harms associated with unaccountable social-media platforms, shouldn't we know better?

item.title: The Israel Lobby’s Responsibility for the Iran War
item.description: The advocates for the U.S.-Israeli special relationship have played a special role.

item.title: Trump’s Iran Quagmire Could Sink America
item.description: It remains to be seen just how much more damage US institutions will suffer because of President Donald Trump's ill-conceived foreign adventure in the Middle East. What is certain is that the threat to US democracy, social stability, and economic resilience is now greater than at any time in living memory.

item.title: High Demand in India Intensifies Timber Smuggling From Myanmar
item.description: Timber worth a whopping $40 million is smuggled from Myanmar to India annually. This is a conservative estimate.

item.title: The Iran War: Its Impact on ASEAN and Myanmar
item.description: A conversation with Michael Martin on the conflict, and how the region should deal with U.S. President Donald Trump.

item.title: The Vulnerability of Trump’s Personality Cult
item.description: Like Mussolini, Stalin, Hitler, the Kims of North Korea, and other famous personality-cult figures, Donald Trump certainly has a knack for staging performances, delivering simple symbolic messages, and putting others in their place. Yet unlike these predecessors, he is ill-equipped to entrench his power for the long term.

item.title: India’s Refusal to Acknowledge Its Racist Prejudices
item.description: Name-calling and violence against youth from the country’s Northeast in India’s cities stem from deep racial prejudice.

item.title: Will the Iran War Derail Colombia’s World-First Energy Transition?
item.description: An economy dependent on fossil fuels planned to wind down, rather than expand, extraction.

item.title: ‘I Was Forced to Shock Them’: Life Inside Cambodia’s Online Scam Compounds
item.description: There's been widespread focus on foreigners trafficked into Cambodia's cyber-scam compounds. But a growing number of Cambodian nationals are being forced to work in the illegal industry as well.

item.title: Price Hikes at the Pump Destabilize Southeast Asian Politics
item.description: Indonesia, for one, has a bloody history of fuel-related riots.

item.title: Europe Cannot Be a Military Power
item.description: Why defense integration could fracture the continent.

item.title: How America's War on Iran Backfired
Malaysia and Indonesia Shouldn’t Ban YouTube for Under-16s
item.description: One of the great storehouses of public information may soon become collateral damage in the region's child social media bans.

item.description: The decision contradicts the values that the U.K. claims to represent, to say nothing of its supposed support for democracy in Myanmar.

item.title: Vietnam Asks Japan, South Korea For Help Accessing Crude Oil Supplies
item.description: With one of the smallest "petroleum buffers" in the region, the Iran war could hit the Vietnamese economy with particular force.

item.description: The meeting follows a controversial multi-phase election that was dominated by the military-backed Union Solidarity and Development Party.

item.description: Shortages of fuels and chemicals threaten industries from farming to pharmaceuticals

item.title: Trump Seeks Help to Reopen Hormuz. Europe Says No.
item.description: After months of bashing allies, the White House finds itself fighting largely alone.

item.title: ‘A Moment of Grave Peril’
item.description: Aid agencies warn that the Iran war will deepen humanitarian crises across the region.

item.title: War may bring lasting change to the airline business
item.description: Western carriers will relish the chance to win some of their customers back

item.title: European Democracy Is Doing Just Fine, Top EU Official Says
item.description: Despite White House claims, the EU’s human rights official argues that “democracy is thriving” on the continent.

item.title: The quiet recovery of Ireland’s ancient tongue
item.description: In the land of St Patrick, Irish is making a steady comeback

item.title: Rapid-charging EV batteries are on the way
item.description: They can be topped up in as little time as a tank of fuel

item.description: They are worried Uncle Sam will abandon them if they don’t fight

item.description: There is no simple way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A Maoist survival guide to the Iranian energy crisis
item.description: For a self-reliant power like China, no supplier is ever irreplaceable

item.title: The Demand Side of Our New Political Reality
item.description: Most commentaries on democratic erosion focus on the supply side of the equation – the strongmen and new doctrines, blocs, or geopolitical arrangements disrupting domestic politics and the rules-based international order. While important, this perspective ignores the demand that is driving current political trends.

item.title: BRICS Meets Reality in the Middle East War
item.description: It’s the latest case study in the persistent failure of transnational solidarity.

item.title: Jürgen Habermas, the Last Rationalist
item.description: The German philosopher built the 20th century’s most rigorous defense of democratic reason and then watched it tested.

item.title: Why China’s OpenClaw Mania Is More Than Just a Tech Craze
item.description: Local governments and tech conglomerates were the deliberate architects of the enthusiasm, while the central government was forced to hit the brakes.

item.title: After the ‘Balen Wave’: The Future of Nepal’s Politics
item.description: The question now is whether the RSP will succumb to the internal factionalism and political inertia that have historically undermined the country's governments.

item.title: Adam Smith on Today’s Global Economy
item.description: Two and a half centuries after its publication, The Wealth of Nations remains a useful lens for understanding the forces driving deglobalization, technological upheaval, and widening inequality. Far from championing unfettered capitalism, Smith would recognize markets’ limits and the need for oversight.

item.title: Afghan Women Share Their Struggles – and Support
item.description: Every story that was shared at a recent online gathering offered a window into the reality of life for women in Afghanistan today.

item.title: How the Iran War Could Boost Russia’s Role in Asia’s Energy Future
item.description: If instability around Hormuz persists, it could accelerate Russia’s long-planned – and long-stalled – “pivot to Asia.”

item.title: France’s Far Right Now Has a Clear Path to Power
item.description: Leftists and nationalists are managing to completely break republicanism.

item.title: The U.S. War on Iran Has Put the Lebanese Government in a Bind
item.description: It can neither protect Lebanon from Israel nor disarm Hezbollah.

item.title: The Feasibility Trap
item.description: It seems increasingly obvious that the behavior of each actor in the Iran war follows directly from what its technology allows it to do. The result is a dynamic in which military decisions are driven less by strategic calculation than by the operational possibilities created by intelligence and innovation.

item.title: Can Central Asia Cash in on the Critical Minerals Rush?
item.description: As the geopolitical race to secure alternative sources for critical minerals heats up, interest in Central Asia’s mining sector has kicked into a new gear.

item.title: North Korea Launches Massive Ballistic Missile Barrage Amid South Korea-US Drills
item.description: The unusual firing of 10 missiles comes as Pyongyang seeks to command attention while global focus remains fractured across multiple international conflicts.

item.title: Refashioning a Strategic Partnership: Canada and China in a Fragmenting World Order
item.description: The Canada-China breakthrough may have made things easier for other middle powers, like the U.K. and Germany, who have also stepped up their links with China recently.

item.title: Iran’s Escalation Strategy Won’t Work
item.description: Tehran’s compulsive gambling has paid off in the past, but this time looks different.

item.title: Pakistan’s Military Campaign in Afghanistan Is Here to Stay
item.description: Pakistan's recent strikes signal that it is prepared to take the fight directly to the Taliban leadership’s inner circle if the Taliban regime’s support for anti-Pakistan groups continues.

item.title: Gulf War 3.0: How Is India Securing Its Oil Supplies?
item.description: India’s limited reserves, thin domestic production capacity, and mounting oil import dependence leave it extremely vulnerable should the war in West Asia continue.

item.title: Iran’s Political Prisoners Are in the Firing Line
item.description: Thousands of detainees are in danger from U.S. and Israeli strikes and Tehran’s vengeance.

item.title: ‘Made in America’ Should Accept Chinese Investment
item.description: Private Chinese capital is being locked out of mutually beneficial opportunities.

item.title: The Hidden Economic Costs of Menopause
item.description: For too long, menopause was rarely discussed and understudied, leaving us with little knowledge of the economic and social costs associated with it. Now that the effects on productivity and public budgets are finally being measured, it is already clear that the long-term toll for society could be very large indeed.

item.title: Is the Ice Finally Breaking in Bangladesh-India Relations?
item.description: Security officials from the two countries met in Delhi days after the BNP government took charge in Dhaka.

item.title: The China–Iran Partnership and the Limits of Beijing’s Non-Interference Alliance Model
item.description: A preference for strategic flexibility has left Beijing dependent on a narrow set of "allies" whose usefulness is limited – especially in times of crisis.

item.title: ASEAN Again Calls for Halt to Iran War, Citing Possible ‘Sustained’ Economic Challenges
item.description: The bloc's economic ministers warned that a protracted conflict could hinder economic progress and impacting the livelihoods of millions across the region.

item.title: From Vietnam to Iran, War Is the Reason Americans Don’t Trust Their Government
item.description: Presidents cannot ignore the long-term costs of dismissing the truth in pursuit of national security.

item.title: How Takaichi Can Triumph
item.description: And what others can learn from Japan's embrace of American power.

item.title: Why Russia Is Watching Iran Burn
item.description: The Kremlin is in no hurry to save its closest partner in the Middle East.

item.title: Good Riddance to Corporate Social Responsibility
item.description: The end of an unnecessary fiction.

item.title: New Thai Parliament Convenes Amid Continuing Legal Scrutiny of February 8 Election
item.description: The Constitutional Court is set to rule on whether the use of barcodes and QR codes on ballot papers violated constitutional provisions on voter confidentiality.

item.title: Winners and Losers in the AI Workplace
item.description: With AI labs continuing to roll out new models and tools geared toward unlocking productivity gains in the workplace, the future of work has become a major economic, social, and political issue. The question, then, is which careers, occupations, and industries stand to gain and lose the most in the near term.

item.title: The Problem With Billionaires
item.description: There are compelling reasons why free, democratic societies should limit the amount of personal wealth any individual can have. Beyond the strong philosophical objections to extreme inequality, empirical research increasingly shows that economies, societies, and the planet would be better off without the ultra-rich.

item.title: How Inequality Caused America's Affordability Crisis
item.description: Recent election cycles have shown that “affordability” is among American voters’ greatest and most persistent concerns. But partly because the basic problem has been misdiagnosed, the widely appealing, commonsense solution to it has failed to gain political traction.

item.title: Lonely Empire
item.description: The Trump administration’s overt imperialism has unraveled the global order of shared norms and institutions faster than anyone expected. We now find ourselves at a juncture where everyone must either follow the US back into the jungle or refuse, leaving the America to its own devices.

item.title: PS Quarterly Interview: Desmond Lachman
item.description: Donald Trump is inadvertently inviting the return of bond-market vigilantes and a crisis in the US Treasury market – developments that could puncture the AI bubble, depress US equity prices, and tip the US economy into recession. But, from Japanese debt to Chinese imbalances, the global economy’s woes extend much further.

item.title: The Real Fallout of Trump’s Tariffs
item.description: Donald Trump might have hoped that his “Liberation Day” tariffs would punish China and boost the competitiveness of American industries, but they have instead had the opposite effect. Worse, the policy has had far-reaching unintended consequences that few policymakers and commentators have yet to reckon with.

item.title: Asian Manufacturing in Flux
item.description: Rising US-China frictions and worsening global imbalances have triggered a fundamental reordering of Asia’s manufacturing landscape. The ongoing redirection of trade and investment flows will likely hollow out traditional manufacturing in countries that fail to get ahead of the problem.

item.title: Money Enters a New Era
item.description: As recently as a century ago, private currencies competed with each other and with government-issued money. Not until the emergence of central banks did the balance shift decisively in favor of fiat currency; and now the pendulum is swinging back – though only partially, and not without introducing new risks.

item.title: Online Age-Gating Is the Wrong Answer
item.description: With social-media minimum-age restrictions – or “bans” – gaining traction globally, it is worth considering whether this popular new policy intervention is as effective as its proponents claim. The evidence suggests not, and even raises concerns that age-gating could do more harm than good.

item.title: Trumponomics Is Failing on Growth
item.description: After campaigning as a businessman who would usher in a new golden age of growth in America, US President Donald Trump's economic track record in 2017 proved disappointing. Despite large tax cuts and little standing in the way of Trump's deregulatory agenda, the US economy has performed no better under his administration than under the last.

item.title: The Bukele Effect in Latin America
item.description: Not surprisingly, Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele’s success in reducing violent crime has led other political leaders across the region to try to take a page from the same playbook. But offering the public a choice between insecurity and authoritarianism is not leadership; it is failure dressed up as resolve.

item.title: Global Markets Are A-Changin’
item.description: The top three trends shaping the global investment terrain suggest that risk assets will be the winners, at least in the short term. But investors should beware, because the same trends also threaten to alter the functionality of capital markets in multiple ways.

item.title: Why the Iran crisis caught Europe flat-footed
item.description: The EU suffers the war’s fallout, but can do little to cut it short

item.description: The conflict in the Middle East accentuates a structural weakness in China’s global strategy: the absence of capable allies that can act as a force multiplier.

item.description: The development of the current conflict has placed Beijing in a complex and awkward strategic position.

item.description: Beijing is embarking on a "whole-of-nation push" to achieve permanent dominance in physical AI technologies.

item.description: Test yourself on the week of March 7: The war in Iran continues, conflict flares in South Sudan, and José Antonio Kast is inaugurated.

item.description: An Oscar-nominated documentary offers an intimate view of Russia’s descent into fascism.

item.title: The Iran War Could Trigger a Global Food Crisis
item.description: While media coverage of Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz has focused on oil prices, the implications for global food supplies are no less alarming. A prolonged closure could disrupt agriculture worldwide and place more than 100 million people at risk of a humanitarian catastrophe.

item.title: Christian Democracy Is Facing Regime Change
item.description: Europe’s unique political tradition is under threat—and perfectly suited for the post-liberal moment.

item.title: Two Books About the Pull of Home
item.description: Plus, more international fiction releases in March.

item.description: Instead of working with locally elected officials in Jammu and Kashmir, New Delhi is drafting legislation to forcibly remove opposition figures from office.

item.description: The evidence is tantalising. But that is not the same as proof

item.title: 5 Streaming Movies to Help Understand Iran Now
item.description: Many of these filmmakers have found themselves scrutinized by government censors, leading to arrests and imprisonment.

item.title: How the Climate Won Paris’s Culture War
item.description: Plenty of issues are on the ballot in this month’s mayoral election. The city’s green transition isn’t one of them.

item.description: The concentration of U.S. military assets in the Middle East is forcing allies like Japan and South Korea to ask themselves hard questions about U.S. reliability.

item.description: What foreign aid cuts mean in practice in one African country.

item.title: Is the Stablecoin Economy Structurally Sound?
item.description: If stablecoins and tokenized assets become systemically important, blockchains will become a form of systemically important infrastructure. But this shift will bring new risks, because not all blockchains are created equal, and they have largely avoided the degree of scrutiny the public expects of critical infrastructure.

item.title: What China’s Five-Year Plan Says About Its Economy
item.description: A growth target of 4.5 percent is the lowest in decades.

item.description: The effort to reduce dependence on Chinese-controlled supply chains is now starting to have tangible results.

item.title: Shockwaves Across Asia: The Iran War’s Strategic Fallout
item.description: From energy disruptions to alliance tensions, a geographically distant conflict is having immediate consequences across the Indo-Pacific.

item.title: Mobilizing Africa’s Capital for African Development
item.description: From growing pools of African savings to expanding global sovereign wealth funds, there is more than enough capital available to close Africa's funding gaps. To mobilize it effectively, development banks must start thinking less like balance-sheet lenders and more like "capital architects."

item.title: Vietnam and the Geopolitics of Critical Minerals
item.description: While Washington builds coalitions, Vietnam is betting on self-reliance.

item.title: India Can Avoid the Middle-Income Trap
item.description: Can India avoid the dreaded middle-income trap that has ensnared so many other developing countries in Latin America and Southeast Asia? Its favorable demographics, steadily improving economic governance and civil administration, and recent trade agreements certainly suggest so.

item.title: Japan’s Political Paradox: Why Young Liberals Backed a Conservative Landslide
item.description: Are young Japanese voting based on an “idol effect” – or are they redefining entirely what it means to be liberal vs conservative?

item.title: South Koreans Keep the Faith in America Even as Washington Tests It
item.description: South Koreans continue to choose the United States over any rival power by historically wide margins. But don't call it naivety.

item.title: Trump’s Summit Offers More Deference Than Details
item.description: Leaders emerged from the “Shield of the Americas” with few policy specifics.

item.title: Why India Is Right to Support the US and Israel in the Iran War
item.description: What good would it do India to hitch itself to the sinking ship of Iran, instead of its actual partners, such as Israel and the UAE?

item.title: The Gulf’s Tough Choices
item.description: For the Gulf Cooperation Council's six member states, strategic patience may be the wisest approach to the Iran war for now. But the longer the war goes on, the more the region’s credibility as a stable global hub erodes – a vulnerability that the Islamic Republic is determined to exploit.

item.title: Lessons From China’s Age-Gated Internet
item.description: Policymakers worldwide are debating the merits of age-gating and strict content controls to protect children online. China’s experience with these tactics shows that even the most intrusive safeguards cannot reliably prevent harm, which suggests that they are not worth the trade-off in civil liberties.

item.title: How the Iran War Could Usher in a New Era of Global Shiite Extremism
item.description: Degrading command and control over proxy groups could make them more dangerous and unpredictable.

item.title: The Spheres-of-Influence Illusion
item.description: For the first time since 1945, the United States, China, and Russia are converging around an authoritarian conception of sovereignty that relies on raw power rather than international law. But history suggests that dividing the world into rival blocs is more likely to produce conflict than stability.

item.title: A New Global Energy Shock
item.description: When the United States and Israel launched their war of choice against Iran, the Islamic Republic pursued the most effective response at its disposal: triggering a spike in global energy prices by attacking Gulf energy infrastructure and blocking the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. While the long-term effects on energy markets remain difficult to predict, US President Donald Trump has good reason to seek an off-ramp from a war opposed by most Americans, including his MAGA base. But with Israel committed to “total victory” and a defiant Islamic Republic now mining the strait, a cascade of global economic disruption appears increasingly likely.

item.title: The Big Picture
Indonesia’s Local Content Requirements Are No Shortcut to Industrialization
item.description: Without more accountability and transparency, LCRs will remain a blunt protectionist instrument rather than a true catalyst for industrial transformation.

item.title: Major Southeast Asian Economies Targeted by New US Trade Probes
item.description: Against the backdrop of the war in Iran, the move is likely to create new points of friction in U.S. relations with the region.

item.title: Why Europe’s Approach to Defending Democracy Is Failing
item.description: Bromides about the “center holding” miss the point.

item.description: In the strait, Iran holds the advantage—and America has no good options.

item.description: How America and Israel solved Iran’s succession problem.

item.description: The region’s divisions helped precipitate U.S. intervention.

item.description: Will the campaign advance U.S. interests in the region or set them back?

item.title: Strategic Stakes Rise for Bangladesh as Iran Targets Gulf Neighbors
item.description: There are practical ways in which Bangladesh could contribute to the security and stability of the Gulf without becoming directly involved in military confrontation.

item.description: The announcement follows the signing of a major security agreement between Jakarta and Canberra last month.

item.description: A breakdown of the tools Iran has to maintain pressure on Trump.

item.description: Tehran escalates Gulf strikes and warns of future attacks on U.S. bases in the region.

item.title: How the New Gulf War Is Impacting Mongolia
item.description: Countries like Mongolia, despite being distant from the conflict, are already feelings its impact on their economic activities, not to mention how the war disrupts future possibilities.

item.title: Trump’s Last-Gasp Foreign Policy
item.description: Ill-considered overreach in Iran and elsewhere signals imminent decline.

item.title: Trump’s Efforts to Defuse the Oil Spike Aren’t Working
item.description: Big reserve releases, promises of escorts, and insurance can’t convince the market that the crisis will be over soon.
